chapters.title: You are your biggest fan
chapters.text:
Relying on yourself has always mattered, yet today it feels as basic as the air you breathe. Whether you are a young founder planning a new company or a professional moving up in your field, self-sufficiency is your starting capital. Maha Abouelenein has spent more than thirty years working as a public relations expert with tech startups and governments, and most of her wins trace back to hard work, long-term thinking, and, above all, depending on herself.

Cheer for yourself the way you would cheer for someone you love. You deserve that kind of energy too.

Her path was far from easy. She cared for her sick parents from her teenage years and built her career even when she was overqualified for her first serious job. Many people would hold back in that situation, yet Abouelenein urges them to take the leap, because the only supporter who will always be there for us is ourselves. That is why self-reliance is Abouelenein's cardinal rule of success, no matter what stands in the way.

Self-reliance means refusing to wait for others to give you permission. As Maha Abouelenein has said, the future belongs to those who back themselves first.

chapters.title: Stay low and take one step ahead
chapters.text:
The first rule of self-reliance is hard work, and you have probably heard it a thousand times. You might think, of course you have to work, but what does hard work actually mean to you? For Abouelenein, it rests on four key principles, which are staying focused and being wise with social media, keeping a low profile, growing connections, and prioritizing the process over the reward.

Plenty of distractions will try to pull you away from your work, and social media is one of the loudest. These platforms are great at stealing your time, and they also send you mixed signals about your potential. Watching favorite bloggers or competitors can damage your motivation, especially when you are just starting. Saying no to social media or other distractions, like hanging out too much, saves your attention for the work and clears out self-doubt, which is vital for self-reliance.

Social media does have a benefit, since it can boost long-term recognition for you or your brand. However, comparing your idea to your competitors instead of focusing on developing it before going public does not help at the start.

Abouelenein remembers her first job as a secretary for Naguib Sawiris, an Egyptian businessman and billionaire. She was overqualified for the role, with strong work experience at a top company and a master's degree. Still, as a newcomer, she kept a low profile, focused on networking, and showed her creativity while learning from Naguib.

Your career must let you learn or earn. Your dream job combines the two.

Once you are focused and guarding your attention, practice favoring the process over the reward. For Abouelenein, rewards come from combining skills with steady application. Without regularly sharpening your craft, you risk getting frustrated or disappointed when you try to reach your goals. Yet if you show up and contribute consistently, even when life gets in the way, you will slowly build the trust and respect of your most important stakeholder, which is yourself.

chapters.title: Creating value through efforts
chapters.text:
Many of us were taught to follow a steady path: school, college, a stable, well-paying job, and then marriage. You may rightfully think, yes, and that scenario was awful. How often do people get stuck on this paved path, unmotivated and unchallenged, waiting for opportunities to come their way? If you feel the fire of ambition burning within you, yearning for release, it is your duty to quit this waiting mode.

Abouelenein felt stuck once too, waiting for opportunities that never came. She had to sit with herself and find out what dampened her fire. What she discovered was that doing the same old tasks at work and following the same routine did not push her to meet new people or go to new places. She did not notice her complacency because she was comfortable, and comfort is not a bad thing, since living a safe, steady life is a challenge nowadays. However, if this sounds familiar, answer these four questions to figure out if you are a waiter: what insecurities do I need to address, what dreams am I not focusing on, what must I stop doing that no longer serves me, and how can I better control my time to focus on my goals.

Regardless of how big of a waiter you are, your next step is to become a creator.

If you struggle to fit in, you were meant to stand out all along.

Taylor Swift is a perfect example. When her producer, Scooter Braun, purchased the rights to all her albums, she did not wait but acted, proceeding to re-record all of her music to profit from it.

Creators bring value to themselves and others. They carry five traits, which are focus on consumers, strong communication, resourcefulness, curiosity, and confidence. And while these traits may sound familiar, let us become value creators together. For this, we will need to be problem solvers, share knowledge, provide support, and sell our vision. Problem solvers spot and fix issues before anyone asks. Sharing knowledge means writing blogs, creating YouTube content, or teaching if you are an expert at something. Providing support means welcoming tough conversations and showing genuine care for clients. Selling your vision means identifying what your target audience values and anticipating their needs before they do, with confidence.

Together with the five essential traits, you are set to escape the waiting mode.

chapters.title: Clear your hard drive for new skills
chapters.text:
Lifelong learning is essential for everyone who wants to succeed in the modern workforce. Yet we rarely discuss its key steps, which are unlearning, relearning, and investing in yourself.

Abouelenein is spot on in saying self-investment is more relevant now than ever, since digital technologies like artificial intelligence and cloud computing demand faster adaptation than in the past. As technologies help automate many processes, you might benefit from learning how to work with ChatGPT or similar chatbots. However, you must unlearn outdated habits, opinions, and behaviors to fit new knowledge, which involves open-mindedness and a willingness to change. For example, parenting children today differs from twenty years ago because of the latest parenting knowledge.

Once your mind clears space for new information, fill it with current field updates. Try these approaches: identify your knowledge gaps and build a plan to fill them. Challenge your assumptions by asking why you hold these beliefs and how they serve you. Surround yourself with people from different backgrounds and perspectives. Try online courses, or something as simple as the Google of the day, where you research something you heard about that day. Reflect on your curiosities, on three things to unlearn, on what you want to learn, and on three ways to start learning.

Relearning from outdated books contradicts the very purpose of learning.

Beyond intellectual growth, Abouelenein considers emotional intelligence crucial for a successful career. Genuine curiosity and sincerity about others can take you far, whether with potential clients or team members. Furthermore, prioritizing trust in relationships can save you from reputation risks and build your brand value or public opinion. Similarly, networking means gaining new, trustworthy connections if you play your cards right. For example, post that you are going to certain events, list people you hope to meet, prepare by listening to their interviews or scrolling through their social media, reach out directly to them and suggest a meeting, and arrive early to approach the people you are interested in. If you are nervous about going alone, ask your friend to keep you company. Even if the person you are looking for does not come, at least you will have a good time together.

chapters.title: Reputation (Your Version)
chapters.text:
When launching a business, reputation might not top your technical checklist. Yet there are more risks tied to reputation today because of the omnipresence of social media. That is why building your reputation matters both offline and online.

Offline reputation takes a lot of internal work. Your employees become unofficial promoters of your brand, so make sure they feel appreciated and engaged. Remember when John F. Kennedy asked a NASA janitor about his work, and he replied he was helping put a man on the moon. Your employees should feel the same, so share critical information, values, and goals across teams. Consider how your company can encourage dialogue through platforms like Slack or WhatsApp to enable both formal and informal communication, create team-building events online or in person, or start regular newsletters about company activities.

As for online reputation management, the biggest risk today is being canceled, whether deserved or not. The internet can blur important context and misrepresent you, and in that case, your job is to apologize, provide more context, and clearly outline your next steps.

Sometimes, it even makes sense to use humor as a tool of apology. When KFC's UK division ran out of chicken in early 2018, causing a supply chain crisis, they released an ad that read, FCK. WE'RE SORRY. That snippet is not the entire ad, but look at how creative they were.

Social media makes companies cautious but closer to customers, so use it wisely.

Personal branding is another crucial part of your reputation package. A solid personal brand grows from uniquely combining experience, skills, and individual traits. Build yours while incorporating these three qualities. Authenticity means being consistent with your online and offline personas while staying honest, but do not sacrifice your personal life to do so. Commitment means keeping your message and values consistent to build audience trust. Purpose means defining what you want to be known for and deciding what your audience should know about you, which is essentially building your narrative.

A 2023 Gallup study found that companies with engaged employees outperform competitors by 202 percent, which is worth keeping in mind.

chapters.title: There are no mistakes, only lessons
chapters.text:
Failures hit like a punch in the throat, do they not. When we have pushed toward a goal or watched a brilliant idea crash, we feel hollow, doubting whether we will ever create something as innovative again, even though we know that time heals most wounds. The middle ground here is finding a balance between grieving the effort spent and learning from mistakes.

Abouelenein had plenty of reasons for regret. As the youngest of two sisters, she cared for her mother, who had multiple sclerosis, and later for her father. She spent her teens and twenties as a caretaker, so when both parents passed away when she was thirty-seven, Abouelenein wondered what her next role would be.

Experience is not as important as how you use it to heal and grow.

Although she was still grieving, she never regretted the time she spent with her family, despite the need to mature faster. Instead, Abouelenein accepted what happened, reflected on it, distilled her lesson, and labeled it all as a valuable experience.

While our experiences may differ from those of others, we should strive for strength. Regret can guide a lot of energy in the wrong direction, such as overthinking our actions instead of fixing current mistakes. Sometimes, we miss important events or speak thoughtlessly, and there is nothing to do but learn from them.

Abouelenein did just that, extracting six principles for living without regret. First, accept that this life is your journey, and do not blame yourself for bad things in your life or business, because they become your lessons and future rewards if you learn from them. Second, learn how to deal with hardships your way, through therapy, a retreat to a secluded place, or jotting your thoughts on paper. Third, do not waste energy on dwelling, since overthinking produces nothing valuable, so learn to step back from the situation to see its actual size. Fourth, be intentional, since your choices and actions should align with your values. Fifth, be creative with your failures, and use your mistakes to think of other ways to solve the situation. Sixth, take accountability, since your responsibility grows with every mistake you resolve successfully.

The goal for you is to take your struggles and turn them into strengths, as Maha Abouelenein has pointed out.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Business is a lot like life if you think about it. On one hand, building a business requires a human touch, whether through reputation, trusted contacts, or solving others' problems. On the other hand, a successful business person is self-reliant and valuable to others. The balance between the two is narrow but possible if you start with yourself. Let us recall the principles of self-reliance. Keep a low profile and focus on the process. Do not wait, create. Unlearn old knowledge and embrace the new. Build reputation through authenticity, consistency, and purpose. Never regret your mistakes, learn from them.

Now that you are equipped with essentials, it is time you build the future of your dreams.

Try this. Learn about networking basics and aim to introduce yourself, engage with people you are interested in, maintain new relationships, and be presentable throughout your day. If launching a business, focus on three things: a strong and clear purpose, a contribution to the community, and a trustworthy product or service. Evaluate your online presence, and remember that your social media presence must represent your values and be as close to your offline persona as possible without compromising your private life.

Read it till the end?
